food_name: Brown Sugar Fresh Milk with Tapioca Pearls (Boba)

local_names.malay: Susu Segar Gula Perang dengan Mutiara Tapioka
local_names.hindi: ब्राउन शुगर ताज़ा दूध टैपिओका पर्ल्स

aliases: Susu Segar Gula Perang dengan Mutiara Tapioka

ब्राउन शुगर ताज़ा दूध टैपिओका पर्ल्स

malay: Susu Segar Gula Perang dengan Mutiara Tapioka
Susu Segar Gula Melaka Perang dengan Mutiara Tapioka
hindi: ब्राउन शुगर ताज़ा दूध टैपिओका पर्ल्स

cuisine: Taiwanese origin; ubiquitous in Singapore bubble tea chains
food_subgroup: Sweetened milk beverage with tapioca pearls; brown sugar syrup and fresh whole milk base — not tea-based

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for brown sugar boba milk. Closest defensible parent is 'sweetened milk beverage' (FOODON:03301737). No exact match exists for the composite drink including tapioca pearls and brown sugar syrup.
ontology.foodex2_description: Milk-based beverages — flavoured milk and milk drinks (FoodEx2 A0CKL). Used as closest defensible parent; the specific brown sugar tapioca variant is not individually coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for brown sugar boba milk. A0CKL (flavoured milk drinks) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific composite beverage. Not clinically coded as a distinct entity.

preparation_method: Brown sugar syrup cooked and drizzled inside cup
Fresh whole milk poured over syrup
main_ingredients: Black tapioca pearls (cooked in brown sugar syrup)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 500 ml cup of brown sugar fresh milk boba, comprising approximately 470 ml fresh whole milk with brown sugar syrup and approximately 50 g cooked black tapioca pearls, weighing approximately 520 g in total after accounting for ice displacement.

nutrient_source.title: USDA SR Legacy — Whole Milk (NDB 01077) and Tapioca (NDB 20069) components; supplemented with Singapore chain nutrition disclosures (Tiger Sugar / LiHo) for brown sugar syrup contribution
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) SR Legacy; Tiger Sugar and LiHo public nutrition disclosures
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Composite drink values derived from USDA SR Legacy whole milk (NDB 01077) and cooked tapioca (NDB 20069) weighted by approximate recipe proportions (~90% milk base, ~10% cooked tapioca pearls by weight), with brown sugar syrup contribution estimated from USDA SR Legacy brown sugar (NDB 19334) at approximately 38–40 g added sugar per 500 ml serving. Per-100g values represent the blended composite. Singapore chain public nutrition panels (Tiger Sugar, LiHo) were used to cross-validate total sugar and energy per serving.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Brown Sugar and Tapioca Starch — University of Sydney GI Database; composite drink GI estimated from component GI values (brown sugar GI ~65, cooked tapioca pearls GI ~56–94 depending on preparation, whole milk GI ~27–34)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite brown sugar fresh milk boba drink. GI of 85 is an analogous estimate derived from University of Sydney GI Database component values: brown sugar GI ~65 (similar to sucrose GI 65); cooked tapioca pearls GI ~56–94 (mean ~75 for commercially prepared pearls cooked in sugar syrup); whole milk GI ~27–34. The composite GI of ~85 reflects the dominant contribution of rapidly absorbed brown sugar syrup and high-GI tapioca starch at full-sugar preparation. Glycemic load of 70 per 520 g serving is calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (85 × 82) / 100 ≈ 70. This is a high-confidence estimate consistent with published boba beverage metabolic studies.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content with indirect cardiovascular and metabolic risk; low direct sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is low at 38 mg/100 g (approximately 198 mg per 520 g serving), posing no direct sodium concern for hypertension; however, the very high added sugar content (approximately 38–40 g brown sugar per cup) contributes to weight gain, insulin resistance, and metabolic dysfunction — all established indirect risk factors for hypertension worsening — and regular consumption should be avoided or strictly limited in hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high glycemic load (GL ~70 per serving) from combined brown sugar syrup and tapioca starch at very high GI (~85)
type2_diabetes.guidance: A single 500 ml cup delivers approximately 82 g total carbohydrate at GI ~85 and GL ~70 — exceeding the total carbohydrate recommended for an entire meal in T2DM management (45–60 g per meal per ADA guidelines) — and should be avoided entirely by patients with Type 2 Diabetes; if consumed, a 0% sugar variant without tapioca pearls reduces carbohydrate to approximately 12–18 g (milk lactose only), which is more manageable but still requires bolus insulin adjustment.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar intake linked to elevated triglycerides, LDL-C, and systemic inflammation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of high-sugar beverages such as this drink (approximately 38–40 g added brown sugar per cup) is associated with elevated serum triglycerides, reduced HDL-C, increased LDL particle density, and elevated hs-CRP — all established cardiovascular risk factors — and the American Heart Association recommends limiting added sugar to 25 g/day for women and 36 g/day for men, thresholds exceeded by a single serving of this drink; patients with established CVD should avoid this beverage.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar is proinflammatory via AGE production, NF-κB activation, and prostaglandin pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Extremely high added sugar (38–40 g per cup) activates proinflammatory pathways including AGE production, NF-κB, and prostaglandin synthesis; regular consumption is associated with elevated hs-CRP and TNF-alpha in RA cohort studies, and RA patients should avoid this beverage or choose the 0% sugar variant without tapioca pearls.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brown Sugar Fresh Milk with Tapioca Pearls (黑糖鲜奶珍珠, Hēitáng Xiānnǎi Zhēnzhū) is the highest-selling and most visually iconic bubble tea variant in Singapore since 2018, popularised by Tiger Sugar (台湾虎记茶) and rapidly adopted by LiHo, Gong Cha, KOI, and dozens of independent chains. The drink is prepared by cooking black tapioca pearls in concentrated brown sugar syrup until glossy and deeply caramelised, then swirling the thick brown sugar syrup dramatically up the inside of a clear plastic cup to create the signature 'tiger stripe' visual pattern before pouring fresh whole milk over the top. The result is a rich, creamy, intensely sweet beverage with a distinctive caramel-molasses flavour from the brown sugar and a chewy, dense texture from the tapioca pearls. Critically, this drink contains NO BREWED TEA — it is purely fresh whole milk, brown sugar syrup, and tapioca pearls — which is clinically significant: there is no caffeine, no tea polyphenols, and no antioxidant benefit from tea. It is the highest-sugar boba variant at major Singapore chains. A single 500 ml cup (approximately 520 g) contains approximately 82 g total carbohydrate, 48–55 g total sugar (38–40 g added brown sugar plus approximately 12 g milk lactose), and approximately 494 kcal per serving. The glycemic index is approximately 85 (high) with a glycemic load of approximately 70 per serving — exceeding the total carbohydrate recommended for an entire meal in T2DM management. A critical patient misconception must be addressed: brown sugar has a GI of approximately 65, essentially identical to white sugar (GI 65), and provides no meaningful nutritional advantage. The tiger stripe visual is purely aesthetic. Per 100 g, the drink provides 95 kcal, 2.8 g protein, 2.5 g fat, 15.8 g carbohydrate, and 10.2 g sugar. Calcium content is approximately 95 mg/100 g (approximately 494 mg per cup), providing meaningful dairy calcium. Sodium is low at 38 mg/100 g. This beverage is not suitable for patients with Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis at full-sugar preparation.
common_user_queries: brown sugar vs white sugar healthier
how much sugar in brown sugar boba

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values are derived from a composite estimate using USDA SR Legacy whole milk and tapioca components weighted by approximate recipe proportions, cross-validated against Singapore chain public nutrition disclosures (Tiger Sugar, LiHo). No single approved primary source provides a direct per-100g analysis of this specific composite drink. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact match exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite beverage.
notes: All core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a whole-milk and tapioca composite beverage. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed trace/negligible for fresh whole milk and tapioca starch per USDA SR Legacy; this is a naturally occurring near-zero value consistent with the source data. Water content not reported (-1) as the composite drink water fraction was not separately calculated from the blended estimate. The 'brown sugar is healthier than white sugar' misconception is clinically important to address: brown sugar GI (~65) is essentially identical to white sugar GI (~65). FoodEx2 code A0CKL is used as the closest defensible parent (flavoured milk drinks); no exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific product. Ontology codes for FoodOn and SNOMED CT are null with documented gap notes — no fabricated codes used.
